passage: Tardigrades, microscopic animals often called water bears, are among the hardiest organisms known. When their watery habitats dry out, tardigrades expel most of the water from their bodies and enter a dormant state called the tun, in which metabolism slows to a virtual standstill. In this state they can survive extreme cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of space. Early researchers assumed that a sugar called trehalose, which some dormant organisms use to protect their cells, was the key to this endurance. More recent work, however, has shown that many tardigrade species produce little trehalose. Instead, these species rely on a set of proteins, unique to tardigrades, that form a glass-like matrix around delicate cellular structures as the animal dries. Because these proteins can be transferred to other cells in the laboratory, some scientists hope they might one day help preserve biological materials such as vaccines without refrigeration.

questions.stem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.A: describe a mechanism that accounts for an organism's unusual endurance and note a possible application of it
options.B: argue that trehalose plays no role in the survival of any dormant organism
options.C: compare tardigrades with other microscopic animals that enter dormancy
options.D: trace the history of research on the vacuum of space
options.E: explain why vaccines currently require refrigeration

questions.explanation: The passage explains how tardigrades survive drying (the protein matrix, correcting the earlier trehalose assumption) and closes with a possible application (preserving vaccines). A captures both.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the tun state is characterized by

options.A: a metabolism that slows to a virtual standstill
options.B: an increase in the animal's water content
options.C: the production of large amounts of trehalose
options.D: a permanent loss of the ability to revive
options.E: the transfer of proteins to other organisms

questions.explanation: The passage states that in the tun state 'metabolism slows to a virtual standstill.' The other options contradict or overstate the text.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the earlier assumption about trehalose was

options.A: incorrect as a general explanation for tardigrade endurance, because many species produce little of it
options.C: correct for every tardigrade species studied
options.D: based on observations of the vacuum of space
options.E: unrelated to the survival of any organism

questions.explanation: Since 'many tardigrade species produce little trehalose,' the earlier assumption cannot be the general explanation. A follows; B, C, D, E overreach or contradict.

questions.stem: The author mentions that the protective proteins can be transferred to other cells primarily in order to

options.A: support the suggestion that the proteins might have practical uses
options.B: prove that trehalose is unnecessary for any organism
options.C: explain why tardigrades expel water when their habitats dry out
options.D: question whether tardigrades can survive radiation
options.E: describe the structure of the tun state

questions.explanation: The transfer-to-other-cells detail sets up the closing point that the proteins 'might one day help preserve biological materials.' Its function is to support that application.

passage: Medieval craft guilds are often portrayed simply as early trade unions that protected workers. Their role, however, was considerably broader. A guild regulated the quality of goods produced in its trade, set standards for training, and controlled who could practice the craft within a town. To many historians, these functions look restrictive, and it is true that guilds limited competition. Yet the same rules that limited entry also guaranteed buyers a predictable level of quality at a time when there were few other ways to verify a craftsman's skill. A customer purchasing a barrel from a guild cooper could reasonably assume it would not leak, because the cooper had passed the guild's examination. Some economic historians therefore argue that guilds, whatever their costs to would-be competitors, lowered the risk of commerce and so helped trade to expand. The debate over whether guilds were on balance helpful or harmful remains unresolved.

questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.A: presenting a more complex view of guilds than the common portrayal allows
options.B: proving that guilds were purely harmful to medieval commerce
options.C: describing the examination a guild cooper had to pass
options.D: arguing that guilds were identical to modern trade unions
options.E: explaining how barrels were manufactured in medieval towns

questions.explanation: The passage opens by noting guilds are 'often portrayed simply' one way, then broadens that view (quality assurance, lowered risk). A captures this corrective purpose.

questions.stem: It can be inferred from the passage that, in the period discussed, buyers

options.A: had limited means of judging a craftsman's skill apart from guild membership
options.B: always preferred goods made outside the guild system
options.C: were legally required to buy from guild members
options.D: could easily test every product before purchase
options.E: distrusted goods that carried a guild's approval

questions.explanation: The passage says there 'were few other ways to verify a craftsman's skill,' implying buyers relied on guild membership as a signal. A follows.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the claim that guilds were merely restrictive can best be described as
questions.explanation: The author concedes guilds limited competition ('it is true') but argues they also lowered commercial risk, disagreeing in a measured way. A ('qualified disagreement') fits.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

options.A: A common view is stated, complicated by additional considerations, and left as an open debate.
options.B: A thesis is stated and then supported by a single extended example.
options.C: Two historians' theories are compared and one is shown to be false.
options.D: A chronological history of guilds is presented from origin to decline.
options.E: A problem is described and a definitive solution is proposed.

questions.explanation: The passage gives the simple portrayal, adds broader functions and the risk-lowering argument, and ends noting the debate is 'unresolved.' A matches.

passage: For most of human history the night sky was a shared feature of experience, but artificial lighting has made truly dark skies rare near cities. The glow of streetlights and buildings scatters in the atmosphere and washes out all but the brightest stars, a phenomenon known as skyglow. Astronomers were the first to raise the alarm, since skyglow degrades observations from ground-based telescopes. In recent decades, however, the concern has widened. Researchers have documented that excessive nighttime light disrupts the behavior of nocturnal animals: migrating birds veer off course, and hatchling sea turtles, which navigate toward the brightest horizon, crawl inland toward roads instead of out to sea. Some studies also link chronic exposure to light at night with disrupted sleep in humans. Advocates for dark skies stress that the remedy is not to abandon outdoor lighting but to use it more thoughtfully—shielding fixtures so light points downward and switching to warmer tones—measures that reduce skyglow while still lighting the ground where people need it.

questions.stem: The main idea of the passage is that

options.A: excessive artificial light causes a range of problems that can be reduced through better lighting practices
options.B: ground-based telescopes should be replaced with space-based ones
options.C: outdoor lighting should be eliminated to protect wildlife
options.D: sea turtles are the species most harmed by artificial light
options.E: skyglow is a concern only for professional astronomers

questions.explanation: The passage catalogs harms from skyglow (astronomy, wildlife, sleep) and ends with thoughtful-lighting remedies. A captures both problem and solution; the others are too narrow or contradicted.

questions.stem: According to the passage, hatchling sea turtles are affected by artificial light because they

options.B: are attracted to warm-toned light specifically
options.C: sleep less when exposed to light at night
options.D: migrate along the same routes as birds

questions.explanation: The passage states the hatchlings 'navigate toward the brightest horizon' and so head inland toward roads. A restates this; the others misattribute the mechanism.

questions.stem: The author mentions migrating birds and sea turtles primarily in order to

options.A: illustrate that the harms of skyglow extend beyond astronomy
options.B: argue that wildlife matters more than human sleep
options.D: prove that outdoor lighting should be abandoned
options.E: describe how warmer light tones are produced

questions.explanation: The birds and turtles are examples introduced after 'the concern has widened,' showing skyglow's reach beyond astronomy. A gives their function.

questions.stem: Which of the following outdoor-lighting choices is most consistent with the advocates' recommendations described in the passage?

options.A: A downward-shielded, warm-toned lamp that illuminates a walkway
options.B: A bright, upward-facing floodlight aimed at the sky
options.C: A cool blue-white light left on all night across an empty field
options.D: The complete removal of all lighting from public streets
options.E: A rotating beacon designed to be visible for many miles

questions.explanation: The advocates favor shielding fixtures to point light downward and using warmer tones while still lighting the ground. A matches; the others increase skyglow or abandon lighting entirely.
